text:
This paper evaluates the alignment between the curriculum of a professional bachelor’s program at a Moroccan university and the 21st-century skills essential for modern graduate employability. Utilizing a mixed-methods research design, the study integrated a comprehensive curriculum review with questionnaires and semi-structured interviews involving 89 students and 9 faculty members. The curriculum evaluation focused on the inclusion of core competencies such as critical thinking, creativity, and digital literacy. Quantitative data from student questionnaires measured perceived readiness for the professional world, while qualitative interviews captured faculty perspectives on pedagogical effectiveness.

The findings reveal a significant discrepancy between instructional intent and student outcomes. While the curriculum analysis and student feedback indicated that course content is largely obsolete and lacks 21st-century skill integration, faculty members maintained that their courses provided sufficient preparation, often attributing student unreadiness to high rates of absenteeism. Most students reported feeling ill-equipped for the labor market, citing a focus on theoretical knowledge rather than practical application. The study concludes that an urgent curriculum revamp is necessary to bridge the gap between academia and industry. It recommends transitioning toward interactive, project-based instruction and fostering metacognitive resilience and self-motivation to support lifelong learning in an evolving global economy.
